name: Yamawaro
description: The Yamawaro is a type of yōkai, or supernatural creature, found in the mountains of western Japan, particularly in the Kyushu region. They are often depicted as childlike beings, resembling kappa but with more earthly qualities. Yamawaro are known for their mischievous nature, and their association with autumn and the harvest. They are said to be the precursors to the kami (spirits or gods) of the rice fields, helping to ensure a bountiful harvest. Unlike kappa, they do not have a water-filled depression on their head, but they are sometimes depicted with a single eye or a bald head. They are believed to possess superhuman strength and are known to be fond of sumo wrestling. Encounters with Yamawaro are often portrayed as humorous or cautionary tales, highlighting the importance of respecting nature and the spirits that inhabit it. They are considered harbingers of good fortune if treated with respect but can bring misfortune if angered. Their connection to the mountains and rice fields makes them integral to the agricultural folklore of rural Japan.
subtitle: Mountain Child of Autumn Harvest
appearance: Yamawaro are typically depicted as small, child-like figures with reddish-brown skin. They often have large heads and a single, piercing eye, although some depictions show them with two eyes. Their hair is often described as being either very short or completely bald. They are usually dressed in simple, rustic clothing, reflecting their connection to the mountains and fields. They are sometimes shown carrying small tools or wearing hats made of leaves or straw.
habitat: Yamawaro inhabit the mountainous regions of western Japan, particularly Kyushu. They are often found near rice paddies, streams, and forests, reflecting their connection to agriculture and nature. They are most active during the autumn harvest season.
behavior: Yamawaro are generally mischievous and playful creatures. They enjoy playing pranks on humans and engaging in sumo wrestling. They are said to be fond of helping farmers with their harvest, but they can also be easily angered if disrespected. They are generally solitary creatures but may gather in small groups during the harvest season.

keyMyths.title: The Sumo Wrestling Match
keyMyths.description: A farmer encounters a Yamawaro and is challenged to a sumo wrestling match. The farmer wins through wit and kindness, earning the Yamawaro's respect and ensuring a good harvest.

keyMyths.title: The Grateful Yamawaro
keyMyths.description: A kind villager helps a Yamawaro in need, and in return, the Yamawaro blesses the villager's rice fields with an abundant harvest, highlighting the importance of compassion.

sources.author: Kazuhiko Komatsu
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of various yōkai in Japanese folklore, including the Yamawaro.
